steps.title: Identify Text 2's position on bumblebee emotions
steps.content: Text 2 acknowledges that bees may engage in play and possibly experience a positive emotional state, but immediately qualifies this: “emotions in insects, if they do indeed exist, are likely very rudimentary.” Any response from Text 2's author must reflect both acceptance of possible play and this strict limitation on the depth of those feelings.

steps.title: Test each choice against Text 2's qualified position
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'By objecting that the bees were actually experiencing a negative feeling akin to stress rather than a positive feeling': Text 2 explicitly says the bees “may engage in play, possibly experiencing some kind of positive emotional state” — it does not argue the feeling was negative. ✗
  • Choice B — 'By arguing that some insects other than bumblebees may be capable of experiencing complex emotional states': Text 2 argues the opposite — insect emotions are rudimentary, not complex; and Text 2 makes no claim about other insect species. ✗
  • Choice C — 'By pointing out that even humans sometimes struggle to have fun while engaging in play': Text 2 says nothing about human play; this is outside the scope of the passage entirely. ✗
  • Choice D — 'By noting that if the bees were truly playing, any positive feelings they may have experienced were probably quite basic': matches Text 2's qualified acceptance of play combined with the claim that insect emotions are “likely very rudimentary” — i.e., probably quite basic. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
